headline: Hybrid Public Key Encryption (RFC 9180) joins the toolkit as pki.hpke.
summary: pki.hpke implements the RFC 9180 HPKE base construction -- the standard encrypt-to-a-public-key primitive behind TLS Encrypted Client Hello, MLS, and Oblivious HTTP. It turns a recipient KEM public key into an encapsulated key plus an AEAD-encrypting context: a KEM layer (encapsulate/decapsulate), an HKDF key schedule with per-suite domain separation, and an AEAD seal/open loop with a per-message nonce derived from a base nonce and a sequence counter. It covers the DHKEM suites P-256, P-521, X25519, and X448; HKDF-SHA256 and HKDF-SHA512; all three AEADs (AES-128-GCM, AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20Poly1305) plus the export-only pseudo-AEAD; and all four modes (base, PSK, auth, auth-PSK). It is pure composition over node:crypto -- no ASN.1, no new codec -- and is proven against the RFC 9180 Appendix A known-answer test vectors: 128 vectors spanning every offered suite, KDF, AEAD, and mode, each asserting the derived encapsulated key, shared secret, key schedule outputs, every encryption, and every export byte-for-byte. An invalid or low-order encapsulated point fails closed as a typed error, never a raw derivation fault. DHKEM(P-384) and HKDF-SHA384 are registered by RFC 9180 but its Appendix A ships no known-answer vector for them, so they are not offered -- a request for either fails closed rather than running crypto no test vector proves; both admit as a one-row registry addition once an authoritative KAT exists. Post-quantum KEMs (ML-KEM and the X-Wing hybrid) are likewise not shipped yet -- their HPKE bindings are specified only by moving drafts with no stable interoperability vector -- but the KEM registry is shaped to admit them as a data-row addition once those specifications stabilize.

Added: pki.hpke.setupS(suiteIds, recipientPublicKey, opts) / pki.hpke.setupR(suiteIds, enc, recipientPrivateKey, opts) establish a sender / recipient HPKE context (RFC 9180 sec. 5.1); the returned context exposes seal(aad, pt) / open(aad, ct) with the sequence-counter nonce and a message-limit guard, and export(exporterContext, L) for the secret-export interface. pki.hpke.seal / pki.hpke.open are the single-shot wrappers (sec. 6). pki.hpke.suites carries the RFC 9180 sec. 7 KEM / KDF / AEAD / MODE code points. Keys are node KeyObjects or serialized bytes; the offered suites are DHKEM P-256, P-521, X25519, and X448, HKDF-SHA256 / HKDF-SHA512, the three AEADs plus export-only, and all four modes. RFC 9180.

The error taxonomy gains HpkeError (hpke/*): a malformed, low-order, or otherwise invalid encapsulated or KEM key (hpke/bad-key, so a Diffie-Hellman that fails during derivation surfaces as a typed error, never a raw fault), an unknown or unsupported ciphersuite code point (hpke/unknown-suite, never a silent default), an unsupported mode (hpke/unknown-mode, so an out-of-registry mode is rejected rather than key-scheduled), inconsistent PSK inputs (hpke/inconsistent-psk), an AEAD tag that does not verify (hpke/open-failed, returning no plaintext), a sequence-number overflow (hpke/message-limit, before any nonce reuse), a seal/open against an export-only suite (hpke/export-only), and a wrong-direction context call (hpke/wrong-role, so a recipient context cannot seal nor a sender context open -- they share a key and base nonce).
